title: AWS credentials leaked in public repository
description: GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning flagged an exposed AWS IAM access key (AKIA3WXYZABCD1234) committed to the public repository 'infra-deploy-prod' by service account svc-cicd@company.com. The credential appeared in plaintext inside terraform/backend.tf as part of PR #2341. GitHub's trusted partner notification reached AWS within 2 minutes of the push event. GuardDuty had no active findings at the time of discovery, indicating the key had not yet been used by an external actor.
detection_method: GitHub Advanced Security — Secret Scanning with AWS trusted partner push notification

timeline.event: GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning detected IAM access key AKIA3WXYZABCD1234 in public repository 'infra-deploy-prod' commit f3a91bc; AWS partner notification triggered automatically
PagerDuty P1 alert escalated to on-call SOC analyst j.chen@company.com; triage channel #inc-2025-1207-006 opened in Slack
Root cause confirmed: svc-cicd service account committed hardcoded IAM key in terraform/backend.tf via PR #2341 (merged 23:56 UTC Dec 6); repo was public since Oct 22 when opened for OSS review
IAM key deactivated in AWS console; CloudTrail Lake query confirmed 0 API calls in 4-hour exposure window; key permanently deleted; repo set to private; git history sanitized with git filter-repo
Replacement IAM key created in Vault, pipeline restored; AWS Trust & Safety notified of zero-exploitation confirmation; incident closed

resolution_summary: The compromised IAM access key AKIA3WXYZABCD1234 was deactivated in the AWS IAM console at 00:18Z. A CloudTrail Lake query scoped to the 4-hour exposure window confirmed zero API calls were made using the key, establishing a clean zero-exploitation window. The key was permanently deleted, 'infra-deploy-prod' was set back to private, and the commit history was sanitized using git filter-repo to prevent credential recovery from repository history. A replacement short-lived credential via OIDC was provisioned to restore the CI/CD pipeline by 04:45Z.
root_cause_analysis: The CI/CD service account svc-cicd operated with a long-lived IAM access key (created 14 months prior, never rotated) that was hardcoded into terraform/backend.tf. A developer included the credentials in PR #2341, intending to test a backend config change locally, and merged without realizing the file was included in the commit. The 'infra-deploy-prod' repository had been set to public 6 weeks earlier to facilitate an external open-source review — the visibility change was never reviewed for credential exposure risk. No pre-commit secret scanning hook was configured on developer workstations, and the GitHub organization lacked push protection enforcement, allowing the commit to proceed without a block.
business_impact: Significant security exposure, potential data loss, reputation damage risk, compliance concerns
remediation_actions: Set IAM access key AKIA3WXYZABCD1234 status to Inactive in AWS IAM console at 00:18Z before completing investigation to stop any in-flight use

Ran CloudTrail Lake query to confirm zero-exploitation: SELECT * FROM cloudtrail_logs WHERE userIdentity.accessKeyId = 'AKIA3WXYZABCD1234' AND eventTime BETWEEN '2025-12-06T23:55:00Z' AND '2025-12-07T00:18:00Z' — returned 0 rows
Permanently deleted the access key from svc-cicd IAM user; rotated all 6 remaining long-lived IAM keys found during the audit
Set 'infra-deploy-prod' GitHub repository visibility to Private pending DevSecOps architecture review
Ran git filter-repo --path terraform/backend.tf --invert-paths and force-pushed to main to excise credential from commit history
Provisioned replacement OIDC-based credential for CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions OIDC provider, avoiding new long-lived key issuance
Notified AWS Trust & Safety with the zero-exploitation CloudTrail evidence; logged SEC-4412 for ISO 27001 compliance audit trail
preventive_measures: Mandatory gitleaks pre-commit hooks deployed to all 47 developer workstations via Jamf MDM within 72 hours of incident
GitHub organization-wide push protection enforced via Advanced Security settings — secret pattern matches now block pushes at source
All 23 CI/CD service account credentials migrated from long-lived IAM keys to OIDC-based role assumption using GitHub Actions OIDC provider
AWS Config rule 'access-keys-rotated' (90-day TTL maximum) enabled across all accounts; non-compliant keys auto-flagged for immediate rotation
Repository visibility changes (private → public) now trigger an automated secret scan and security team notification via AWS EventBridge
security_controls_updated: GitHub organization policy updated: push protection and secret scanning enabled for all repositories including private
AWS IAM policy updated to deny CreateAccessKey for service accounts; EC2 instance profiles and OIDC roles now mandatory for automations
HashiCorp Vault dynamic secrets engine configured for AWS — all CI/CD jobs now receive short-lived, auto-rotated credentials
SIEM alert rule added: any AWS IAM key with age > 90 days triggers a severity-medium ticket for forced rotation review
documentation_updated: Incident Response Runbook §4.2 (IAM Credential Exposure) updated with CloudTrail Lake query template and sub-5-minute deactivation SLA
Developer Security Guidelines updated with 'Never Hardcode Credentials' section and gitleaks setup walkthrough
CI/CD Credential Management Procedure document created: covers OIDC setup, Vault integration, and emergency key rotation steps
SEC-4412 Jira ticket archived as compliance evidence for ISO 27001 A.9.4 (Secure Authentication) control
lessons_learned: Long-lived IAM access keys create systemic risk that compounds over time: six additional service accounts were found holding keys older than 90 days during the post-incident audit, none of which had ever been rotated. The 2-minute detection time from GitHub's partner notification is excellent, but the 18-minute gap between alert and key deactivation must be reduced — an automated Lambda trigger on GitHub webhook events should deactivate the key within 2 minutes of detection without requiring human intervention. Repository visibility changes represent an uncontrolled blast radius for secret exposure; any repo made public must be scanned for secrets before the visibility change commits.
follow_up_tasks: SEC-4415: Complete OIDC migration for all 23 CI/CD service accounts — Owner: Platform Engineering, Due: 2025-12-21
SEC-4416: Build automated IAM key deactivation Lambda triggered by GitHub secret scanning webhook (target: <2 min deactivation) — Owner: Cloud Security, Due: 2026-01-10
SEC-4417: Enable AWS Config rule 'access-keys-rotated' on all 4 AWS accounts and remediate 6 non-compliant keys — Owner: Cloud, Due: 2025-12-14
SEC-4418: Add credential hygiene module to DevSecOps onboarding training — Owner: Security Training, Due: 2026-01-20
resolution: AWS IAM access key AKIA3WXYZABCD1234 deactivated and deleted; zero-exploitation confirmed via CloudTrail; repository history sanitized; OIDC-based replacement deployed
action_taken: Resolved by security-team within 295 minutes of GitHub Advanced Security detection
detection_gap: No pre-commit hook or GitHub push protection policy was enforced to block secrets from being committed to repositories; the GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning workflow ran on-push but only alerted 6 minutes after the commit, leaving the AWS access key publicly accessible in a public repository for that window
what_went_well: AWS CloudTrail confirmed zero API calls using the exposed access key during the approximately 6-minute public exposure window — the key was rotated before any observed usage
GitHub Advanced Security flagged the commit within 6 minutes of the push, providing rapid detection despite the absence of push protection
IAM key rotation was executed seamlessly with zero impact to the analytics-etl pipeline — new key was provisioned and deployed while old key was revoked
what_went_wrong: Developer committed AWS credentials directly in a configuration file without a pre-commit hook to scan for and block secrets
GitHub push protection (secret scanning push protection mode) was not enabled — it would have blocked the push at the git protocol level before the commit was accepted
No automated IAM key rotation policy existed; the exposed key had been static for 7 months with no rotation schedule
